refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 requires quantification of the availability target and clarification of measurement scope. Link availability is a system-level quality attribute that depends on network infrastructure, redundancy mechanisms, and fault detection/recovery. The refined FR explicitly defines the metric (availability ratio), threshold (≥0.999), and measurement period to enable objective verification.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculation of cumulative downtime; comparison against 0.999 threshold. Requires instrumentation of link health status and timestamped event logging.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link failures within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate failover or graceful degradation upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall log link state events with timestamps for availability calculation.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The network infrastructure shall provide redundant communication paths or recovery mechanisms to meet availability targets.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dual-path network architecture (primary + backup link) with automatic failover and health monitoring at the Teleoperation module level.
DP-B: Implement network-level redundancy (carrier SLA + local mesh backup) with link aggregation and transparent failover managed by Network Link infrastructure.
DP-C: Implement application-level link availability management with periodic health checks, timeout-based detection, and operator notification of degraded state without automatic failover.
DP-D: Implement hybrid approach combining network redundancy (DP-B) with application-level monitoring and graceful degradation (DP-C) to achieve 0.999 availability.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement hybrid approach combining network-level redundancy (dual carriers or mesh backup with transparent failover) managed by Network Link infrastructure, coupled with application-level link health monitoring, failure detection (target <500ms), and graceful degradation logic in the Teleoperation module. Safety-critical commands shall be queued and retried; non-critical telemetry shall degrade gracefully during transient link loss.

potential_sources: Coupling between Teleoperation module (application-level detection/degradation) and Network Link (infrastructure-level redundancy) is acceptable and necessary; both are required to achieve 0.999 availability.
Coupling between Teleoperation and Safety Services (safety-critical command validation during link loss) is acceptable; Safety Services must validate queued commands upon link recovery.
No undesirable coupling to Vehicle module; Vehicle executes commands received from Teleoperation regardless of link state.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D distributes responsibility appropriately: Network Link owns redundancy and transparent failover; Teleoperation owns application-level health monitoring and graceful degradation logic. This separation of concerns reduces coupling and allows independent optimization of each layer.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network Link infrastructure can provide dual-path or mesh backup with <100ms failover latency.
ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation module can detect link loss within 500ms via periodic health checks (e.g., heartbeat messages).
ASSUMPTION: Safety Services can validate and execute queued safety-critical commands upon link recovery without loss of safety integrity.
RISK: If Network Link failover latency exceeds 500ms, combined detection + failover time may exceed acceptable command timeout, causing command loss.
RISK: Graceful degradation of telemetry during link loss may reduce operator situational awareness; Safety Manager must approve acceptable degradation modes.
RISK: Queuing of safety-critical commands during link loss requires careful state management to prevent command reordering or duplication upon recovery.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to specify: (1) Network Link failover latency target (<100ms), (2) Teleoperation health check interval and timeout threshold (e.g., 3 missed heartbeats = link loss), (3) Safety Services command validation and deduplication logic upon link recovery, (4) Telemetry degradation modes and operator notification strategy. Engage Safety Manager to approve graceful degradation modes for safety-critical telemetry.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Application-level link health monitoring, failure detection, graceful degradation logic, command queuing/retry, and coordination with Safety Services. Excludes network infrastructure (owned by Network Link) and vehicle control logic (owned by Vehicle).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands from tele-operator; Teleoperation module queues commands during link loss and executes upon recovery.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Sends vehicle state and sensor data to tele-operator; degrades gracefully (reduced frequency or subset of data) during link loss.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints from Safety Services; sends safety-critical command validation requests and link loss notifications.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Receives link state events (up/down) and failover notifications from Network Link; sends health check messages and monitors link availability metrics.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of application-level link health monitoring, failure detection, and graceful degradation. High containment; minimal diffusion. External dependency on Network Link for infrastructure-level redundancy is acceptable and necessary.

Network Link owns infrastructure-level redundancy and failover, but application-level monitoring and graceful degradation logic should reside in Teleoperation. If all link availability logic is pushed to Network Link, it becomes overly complex and couples application concerns to network infrastructure.

Safety Services should NOT own link availability logic; it owns safety validation and constraint enforcement. Assigning link availability to Safety Services would diffuse safety concerns and create inappropriate coupling.

Vehicle module owns vehicle control and actuation; it should not own teleoperation link management. Assigning link availability to Vehicle would create inappropriate coupling and diffuse teleoperation concerns.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container; link availability is a system-level quality attribute but should be decomposed to specific modules (Teleoperation + Network Link) rather than owned at the solution level.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module owns application-level link health monitoring, failure detection (<500ms), graceful degradation logic, and command queuing/retry. Network Link module owns infrastructure-level redundancy and failover (<100ms). Safety Services validates safety-critical commands upon link recovery. This distribution achieves high containment (0.95 for Teleoperation) and minimizes diffusion.
containment_risks: RISK: If Teleoperation and Network Link do not coordinate failover timing, combined latency may exceed acceptable command timeout.
RISK: If graceful degradation modes are not clearly defined and approved by Safety Manager, operator may misinterpret degraded telemetry as system failure.
RISK: If command queuing/retry logic is not carefully implemented, safety-critical commands may be duplicated or reordered upon link recovery.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish clear interface contract between Teleoperation and Network Link: (1) Network Link guarantees failover latency <100ms and notifies Teleoperation of link state changes via Network Link interface, (2) Teleoperation implements health check interval and timeout logic to detect link loss within 500ms, (3) Safety Services provides command validation API for Teleoperation to call upon link recovery. Document graceful degradation modes and obtain Safety Manager approval.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid network + application-level redundancy) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 link availability). DP-D is traceable, measurable, and decomposable into sub-DPs for network redundancy, health monitoring, and graceful degradation. No unresolvable coupling or contradictions identified.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK: If application-level link monitoring logic is scattered across Teleoperation and Network Link without clear ownership, maintenance and testing become difficult. Mitigation: Establish clear interface contract and ownership boundaries.

DIFFUSION RISK: If graceful degradation modes are not documented and approved by Safety Manager, different stakeholders may have conflicting expectations. Mitigation: Document degradation modes and obtain explicit approval.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score for application-level link availability logic. Network Link module owns infrastructure-level redundancy. This distribution is appropriate and minimizes diffusion. No undesirable diffusion to Vehicle or Safety Services identified.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via continuous monitoring of link state transitions and calculation of availability ratio during evaluation period. Requires instrumentation of link health status and timestamped event logging in Teleoperation module. Network Link must provide link state notifications and failover metrics.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns application-level monitoring and graceful degradation; Network Link owns infrastructure-level redundancy; Safety Services validates safety-critical commands upon recovery. Ownership boundaries are well-defined and traceable to IM modules.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify Network Link failover latency target (<100ms) and Teleoperation health check interval/timeout (e.g., 3 missed heartbeats = link loss).
Establish interface contract between Teleoperation and Network Link for link state notifications and failover coordination.
Document graceful degradation modes (e.g., reduced telemetry frequency, subset of sensor data) and obtain Safety Manager approval.
Define command queuing/retry logic in Teleoperation to prevent duplication or reordering upon link recovery.
Specify Safety Services command validation API for Teleoperation to call upon link recovery.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it requires decomposition into sub-FRs addressing network redundancy, health monitoring, failure detection, graceful degradation, and safety-critical command handling. DP-D is sound and traceable to Teleoperation IM, but further refinement is needed to specify failover latency targets, health check intervals, and graceful degradation modes. Proceed to next decomposition level to detail sub-FRs and sub-DPs.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link failures within 500 milliseconds via periodic health checks.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate graceful degradation (reduced telemetry frequency or subset of data) upon link loss and resume full telemetry upon link recovery.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall queue safety-critical commands during link loss and execute them upon link recovery with validation by Safety Services.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The network infrastructure shall provide redundant communication paths (dual carriers or mesh backup) with automatic failover latency <100 milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.5: The teleoperation system shall log link state events with timestamps to enable calculation of link availability ratio for verification.

assumptions: Network Link infrastructure can provide dual-path or mesh backup with <100ms failover latency.
Teleoperation module can detect link loss within 500ms via periodic health checks (e.g., heartbeat messages).
Safety Services can validate and execute queued safety-critical commands upon link recovery without loss of safety integrity.
Graceful degradation of telemetry during link loss is acceptable to Site Manager and Safety Manager.
Evaluation period for link availability measurement is defined by customer (e.g., 30 days, 90 days).
open_risks: RISK: If Network Link failover latency exceeds 500ms, combined detection + failover time may exceed acceptable command timeout, causing command loss.
RISK: Graceful degradation of telemetry during link loss may reduce operator situational awareness; Safety Manager must approve acceptable degradation modes.
RISK: Queuing of safety-critical commands during link loss requires careful state management to prevent command reordering or duplication upon recovery.
RISK: If link availability target (0.999) is not achievable with available network infrastructure, customer may require revision of acceptance criteria or investment in additional redundancy.
evidence_needed: Network Link failover latency specification and test results.
Teleoperation health check interval and timeout threshold specification.
Safety Services command validation and deduplication logic specification.
Graceful degradation modes documentation and Safety Manager approval.
Link availability measurement methodology and instrumentation specification.
Customer acceptance criteria confirmation (0.999 target and evaluation period).
